agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Choreography-based saga with an orchestrator for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). Each step has a compensating action. An Orchestrator service drives the forward flow and triggers compensations in reverse order on failure, using a persistent saga log for crash recovery.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga forward step-by-step, tracks saga state, triggers compensating transactions in reverse on failure, handles retries and idempotency","boundaries":"Owns the saga execution log and state machine. Does NOT own any business data — only coordinates."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), confirms orders (CONFIRMED), cancels orders (CANCELLED) as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Exposes createOrder and cancelOrder endpoints."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory for order items, releases reservations as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns inventory stock and reservation records. Exposes reserveItems and releaseReservation endpoints."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges the customer for the order total, issues refunds as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns payment transactions. Exposes chargeCustomer and refundPayment endpoints."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order, unassigns courier as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns courier availability and assignment records. Exposes assignCourier and unassignCourier endpoints."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"createOrder(orderId, items, customerId) → {orderId, status: PENDING}; cancelOrder(orderId) → {status: CANCELLED}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"reserveItems(orderId, items[]) → {reservationId}; releaseReservation(reservationId) → {status: RELEASED}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"chargeCustomer(orderId, customerId, amount) → {paymentId}; refundPayment(paymentId) → {status: REFUNDED}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"assignCourier(orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {courierId}; unassignCourier(orderId, courierId) → {status: UNASSIGNED}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order → Saga Orchestrator creates saga record (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator sends createOrder command → Order Service replies success/fail. 3. On success (state: ORDER_CREATED), sends reserveItems → Inventory Service replies. 4. On success (state: ITEMS_RESERVED), sends chargeCustomer → Payment Service replies. 5. On success (state: PAYMENT_CHARGED), sends assignCourier → Courier Service replies. 6. On success (state: COURIER_ASSIGNED), saga completes → confirms order. On ANY failure, orchestrator walks backward through completed steps, issuing compensating commands in reverse order. Each step transition is persisted to the saga log before the command is sent (write-ahead).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration-based Saga","rationale":"A central orchestrator provides explicit control flow, making the compensation sequence deterministic and easy to reason about. With 4 sequential steps and strict reverse-compensation requirements, orchestration is simpler to debug and monitor than choreography, where compensation logic would be scattered across services."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Each saga instance is modeled as a finite state machine with well-defined forward and compensating transitions. This makes crash recovery trivial — on restart, read the last persisted state and resume from there."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every command (forward and compensating) must be idempotent. The orchestrator may retry on timeout or after crash recovery. Idempotency keys (saga ID + step) prevent duplicate side effects."},{"pattern":"Write-Ahead Saga Log","rationale":"Before sending any command, the orchestrator persists the intended state transition. If it crashes mid-step, recovery reads the log and either retries the command or triggers compensation, preventing stuck sagas."},{"pattern":"Semantic Lock / Pending State","rationale":"Each service marks resources in an intermediate state (e.g., inventory RESERVED not SOLD, order PENDING not CONFIRMED) during the saga. This signals to other transactions that the data is part of an in-flight saga and prevents dirty reads."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED | CANCELLED, contains OrderLineItems)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockItem (root: skuId, tracks available/reserved quantities)","Reservation (root: reservationId, tied to orderId, states: ACTIVE → RELEASED | CONSUMED)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: paymentId, tied to orderId, states: CHARGED → REFUNDED | SETTLED)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment (root: assignmentId, ties orderId to courierId, states: ASSIGNED → UNASSIGNED | COMPLETED)","Courier (root: courierId, availability status)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Saga Orchestrator","relationship":"Open Host Service — Order Service exposes commands the orchestrator consumes"},{"upstream":"Saga Orchestrator","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator dictates when to reserve/release; Inventory complies"},{"upstream":"Saga Orchestrator","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator dictates when to charge/refund"},{"upstream":"Saga Orchestrator","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator dictates when to assign/unassign"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration-based saga over choreography-based saga","gains":"Single place to understand the entire flow. Easier to add steps, reorder steps, debug failures, and implement monitoring dashboards. Compensation order is explicit and deterministic.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and a single point of failure (mitigated by persistence and restart). Slightly more coupling — orchestrator knows about all participants.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and the next service reacts. Pro: no central coordinator, fully decoupled. Con: compensation logic scattered across 4 services, harder to reason about failure sequences, risk of missing an event handler leading to stuck state.","Hybrid: orchestration for the happy path, choreography for compensation. Rejected — unnecessary complexity for 4-step linear flow."]},{"decision":"Async message queue over synchronous HTTP calls","gains":"Temporal decoupling — services don't need to be up simultaneously. Natural retry semantics with dead-letter queues. Backpressure handling.","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (message broker). Eventual consistency — client doesn't get instant confirmation. Requires correlation IDs and reply channels.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous HTTP chain: simpler to implement initially but creates tight temporal coupling, cascading timeout failures, and no built-in retry. Rejected for production use."]},{"decision":"Saga log with write-ahead persistence","gains":"Crash recovery without data loss. Can reconstruct saga state after orchestrator restart. Audit trail for debugging.","sacrifices":"Write amplification — every state transition requires a durable write before proceeding. Adds latency per step (mitigated by fast local storage or append-only log).","alternatives_considered":["In-memory state only: faster but loses saga state on crash, leading to orphaned reservations and charges. Unacceptable."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a linear chain. Adding step 5 (e.g., Notification Service) requires adding one forward state, one compensating state, and wiring the new service. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Retry policies per step","evolution_path":"Each step in the orchestrator can have independent retry count, backoff strategy, and timeout. Payment retries might be more aggressive than courier assignment retries."},{"area":"Parallel step execution","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow steps 2 and 3 (inventory + payment) to run in parallel, the state machine can be refactored to a fork-join pattern. The orchestrator design accommodates this without changing participant services."},{"area":"Saga participant replacement","evolution_path":"Swapping the Payment Service (e.g., from Stripe to Adyen) requires only updating the Payment Service behind the same command interface. The orchestrator and other participants are unaffected."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation order must be strict reverse","reason":"Business rule: undo in reverse order. Charging before reserving or refunding before releasing would violate invariants. The state machine encodes this ordering and it must not be violated."},{"area":"Idempotency contract on all participants","reason":"Every participant MUST handle duplicate commands safely. This is a non-negotiable architectural constraint — without it, crash recovery and retries produce corrupted state."},{"area":"Saga log durability","reason":"The saga log must survive process crashes. Using an in-memory-only log would make the entire pattern unsafe. This is a hard infrastructure requirement."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure — a compensating action itself fails (e.g., refund API is down)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Retry compensating actions with exponential backoff. After max retries, move to dead-letter queue and alert operations team for manual resolution. Never silently drop a compensation."},{"risk":"Saga stuck in intermediate state due to participant being permanently unavailable","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga timeout — if a saga hasn't progressed in N minutes, force-trigger compensation. Expose a dashboard showing in-flight sagas and their age."},{"risk":"Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure","severity":"high","mitigation":"Run multiple orchestrator instances with saga log partitioned by orderId. Only one instance processes a given saga at a time (leader election or queue-based exclusive consumer). On crash, another instance picks up from the persisted state."},{"risk":"Out-of-order message delivery causes incorrect state transitions","severity":"medium","mitigation":"State machine rejects invalid transitions. Each reply includes the saga step ID. The orchestrator validates that the reply matches the expected current step before transitioning."},{"risk":"Phantom reservation — inventory reserved but saga abandoned without compensation","severity":"high","mitigation":"Reservations carry a TTL. If not confirmed within the TTL, the Inventory Service auto-releases them. This acts as a safety net independent of the saga orchestrator."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga as an explicit state machine in the Orchestrator service. The state machine definition should be declarative — a data structure mapping (currentState, event) → (nextState, command). Store the saga log in a durable store (Postgres, DynamoDB, or Redis with AOF). Use a message broker (RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka) for async commands and replies. Each participant service exposes two operations: a forward action and a compensating action, both idempotent. The saga state machine has these states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → ITEMS_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COURIER_ASSIGNED → COMPLETED, with compensating states: COURIER_UNASSIGNING → PAYMENT_REFUNDING → ITEMS_RELEASING → ORDER_CANCELLING → FAILED. On any forward step failure, the machine transitions to the corresponding compensating entry point and walks backward.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/orchestrator/saga-state-machine.ts — Declarative state machine definition with forward and compensating transitions","src/orchestrator/saga-executor.ts — Executes the state machine: sends commands, handles replies, persists state","src/orchestrator/saga-log.repository.ts — Persistence layer for saga instances and their state history","src/order-service/handlers/create-order.handler.ts — Handles createOrder command, returns success/fail reply","src/order-service/handlers/cancel-order.handler.ts — Compensating action handler","src/inventory-service/handlers/reserve-items.handler.ts — Handles reserveItems, ensures idempotency via reservationId","src/inventory-service/handlers/release-reservation.handler.ts — Compensating action handler","src/payment-service/handlers/charge-customer.handler.ts — Handles chargeCustomer with idempotency key","src/payment-service/handlers/refund-payment.handler.ts — Compensating action handler","src/courier-service/handlers/assign-courier.handler.ts — Handles assignCourier","src/courier-service/handlers/unassign-courier.handler.ts — Compensating action handler","src/shared/saga-commands.ts — Command and reply type definitions shared across services"],"patterns_to_follow":"1. Every command includes a sagaId and stepId for correlation and idempotency. 2. Every participant checks if it already processed a given (sagaId, stepId) before executing — if yes, return the cached result. 3. The orchestrator persists state BEFORE sending a command (write-ahead). 4. Compensating actions are best-effort-until-success — retry indefinitely with backoff, never skip. 5. Use dead-letter queues for poison messages that repeatedly fail processing. 6. Each service owns its own database — no shared databases between services.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not use distributed transactions (2PC) — they don't scale across independent services and create tight coupling","Do not skip the saga log persistence — in-memory-only state leads to orphaned side effects on crash","Do not assume compensating actions always succeed on first try — always implement retries","Do not share a database between services — this defeats the purpose of service independence and makes the saga unnecessary","Do not use synchronous HTTP chains for the saga — a single slow or down service blocks the entire flow and makes timeout handling extremely complex","Do not forget to handle the case where the orchestrator crashes between persisting state and sending the command — on recovery, re-send the command (idempotency makes this safe)","Do not let reservations live forever — always pair them with a TTL as a safety net"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration over choreography for a linear 4-step saga with strict compensation ordering","State machine design with write-ahead logging for crash recovery","Idempotency as a non-negotiable requirement for all participants","Async messaging over synchronous HTTP for inter-service saga communication"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific message broker choice (depends on existing infrastructure — Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS have different trade-offs for this use case)","Whether courier assignment should have automatic timeout-based unassignment like inventory has TTL-based release","Exact retry policies and timeout values — these need to be tuned based on observed service latencies in production"],"assumptions":["Each of the 4 services has its own independent data store","A message broker is available or can be introduced","The flow is strictly sequential (no parallel steps in the current requirements)","Compensating actions are semantically reversible — e.g., a payment can always be refunded, a courier can always be unassigned","Order volumes are moderate enough that a single saga orchestrator (with HA replicas) is sufficient — no need for partitioned orchestration at this scale"]}}

content.thinking:
Let me design a saga pattern for this courier marketplace order flow.

Classification: `none` — sufficient constraints exist. The requirements are clear: 4 services, sequential steps, compensating transactions needed on failure.

Eventual consistency — client doesn't get instant confirmation. The orchestrator and other participants are unaffected."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation order must be strict reverse","reason":"Business rule: undo in reverse order. Charging before reserving or refunding before releasing would violate invariants. The state machine encodes this ordering and it must not be violated."},{"area":"Idempotency contract on all participants","reason":"Every participant MUST handle duplicate commands safely. This is a non-negotiable architectural constraint — without it, crash recovery and retries produce corrupted state."},{"area":"Saga log durability","reason":"The saga log must survive process crashes. Using an in-memory-only log would make the entire pattern unsafe. This is a hard infrastructure requirement."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure — a compensating action itself fails (e.g., refund API is down)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Retry compensating actions with exponential backoff. After max retries, move to dead-letter queue and alert operations team for manual resolution. Never silently drop a compensation."},{"risk":"Saga stuck in intermediate state due to participant being permanently unavailable","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga timeout — if a saga hasn't progressed in N minutes, force-trigger compensation. Expose a dashboard showing in-flight sagas and their age."},{"risk":"Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure","severity":"high","mitigation":"Run multiple orchestrator instances with saga log partitioned by orderId. Only one instance processes a given saga at a time (leader election or queue-based exclusive consumer). On crash, another instance picks up from the persisted state."},{"risk":"Out-of-order message delivery causes incorrect state transitions","severity":"medium","mitigation":"State machine rejects invalid transitions. Each reply includes the saga step ID. The orchestrator validates that the reply matches the expected current step before transitioning."},{"risk":"Phantom reservation — inventory reserved but saga abandoned without compensation","severity":"high","mitigation":"Reservations carry a TTL. If not confirmed within the TTL, the Inventory Service auto-releases them. This acts as a safety net independent of the saga orchestrator."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga as an explicit state machine in the Orchestrator service. The state machine definition should be declarative — a data structure mapping (currentState, event) → (nextState, command). Store the saga log in a durable store (Postgres, DynamoDB, or Redis with AOF). Use a message broker (RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka) for async commands and replies. Each participant service exposes two operations: a forward action and a compensating action, both idempotent. The saga state machine has these states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → ITEMS_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COURIER_ASSIGNED → COMPLETED, with compensating states: COURIER_UNASSIGNING → PAYMENT_REFUNDING → ITEMS_RELEASING → ORDER_CANCELLING → FAILED. trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Choreography-based saga with an orchestrator for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). Each step has a compensating action. An Orchestrator service drives the forward flow and triggers compensations in reverse order on failure, using a persistent saga log for crash recovery.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga forward step-by-step, tracks saga state, triggers compensating transactions in reverse on failure, handles retries and idempotency","boundaries":"Owns the saga execution log and state machine. Does NOT own any business data — only coordinates."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), confirms orders (CONFIRMED), cancels orders (CANCELLED) as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Exposes createOrder and cancelOrder endpoints."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory for order items, releases reservations as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns inventory stock and reservation records. Exposes reserveItems and releaseReservation endpoints."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges the customer for the order total, issues refunds as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns payment transactions. Exposes chargeCustomer and refundPayment endpoints."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order, unassigns courier as a compensating action","boundaries":"Owns courier availability and assignment records. On success (state: ORDER_CREATED), sends reserveItems → Inventory Service replies. 4. On success (state: ITEMS_RESERVED), sends chargeCustomer → Payment Service replies. 5. On success (state: PAYMENT_CHARGED), sends assignCourier → Courier Service replies. 6. On success (state: COURIER_ASSIGNED), saga completes → confirms order. This makes crash recovery trivial — on restart, read the last persisted state and resume from there."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every command (forward and compensating) must be idempotent. The orchestrator may retry on timeout or after crash recovery. Idempotency keys (saga ID + step) prevent duplicate side effects."},{"pattern":"Write-Ahead Saga Log","rationale":"Before sending any command, the orchestrator persists the intended state transition. If it crashes mid-step, recovery reads the log and either retries the command or triggers compensation, preventing stuck sagas."},{"pattern":"Semantic Lock / Pending State","rationale":"Each service marks resources in an intermediate state (e.g., inventory RESERVED not SOLD, order PENDING not CONFIRMED) during the saga. The state machine definition should be declarative — a data structure mapping (currentState, event) → (nextState, command). Store the saga log in a durable store (Postgres, DynamoDB, or Redis with AOF). Use a message broker (RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka) for async commands and replies. Each participant service exposes two operations: a forward action and a compensating action, both idempotent. The saga state machine has these states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → ITEMS_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COURIER_ASSIGNED → COMPLETED, with compensating states: COURIER_UNASSIGNING → PAYMENT_REFUNDING → ITEMS_RELEASING → ORDER_CANCELLING → FAILED.
